toolResult.textResultForLlm:
1. ## Build Performance Report
2. 
3. We profiled our project and found that `ResolveProjectReferences` dominates the
4. Target Performance Summary, consuming ~70% of the total build time for the App project.
5. 
6. The team is considering ways to optimize or eliminate this target. Some ideas
7. discussed include:
8. - Removing project references and using NuGet packages instead
9. - Trying to skip ResolveProjectReferences with a custom target override
10. - Consolidating all projects into one
11. 
12. We'd like an expert analysis of whether ResolveProjectReferences is actually the
13. bottleneck, and if so, what to do about it.
14.

---
name: resolve-project-references
description: "Guide for interpreting ResolveProjectReferences time in MSBuild performance summaries. Only activate in MSBuild/.NET build context. Activate when ResolveProjectReferences appears as the most expensive target and developers are trying to optimize it directly. Explains that the reported time includes wait time for dependent project builds and is misleading. Guides users to focus on task self-time instead. Do not activate for general build performance -- use build-perf-diagnostics instead."
license: MIT
---

# Misleading ResolveProjectReferences Time

Prevent misguided optimization of `ResolveProjectReferences` by explaining that its reported time is wall-clock wait time, not CPU work.

## When to Use

- `ResolveProjectReferences` appears as the most expensive target in the Target Performance Summary
- A developer is trying to optimize `ResolveProjectReferences` directly
- Build performance analysis shows a single target consuming 50-80% of total build time

## When Not to Use

- General build performance optimization (use `build-perf-diagnostics` instead)
- The bottleneck is clearly a different target (e.g., `Csc`, `ResolveAssemblyReference`)
- The user has not yet captured a binlog or performance summary

## Inputs

| Input | Required | Description |
|-------|----------|-------------|
| Build log or binlog | Yes | A diagnostic build log or binlog containing the Target Performance Summary |

## Workflow

### Step 1: Confirm the misleading symptom

Verify that `ResolveProjectReferences` appears as the top target in the **Target** Performance Summary. This is the misleading metric.

### Step 2: Explain why it is misleading

The reported time includes **waiting for dependent projects to build** while the MSBuild node is yielded (see dotnet/msbuild#3135). During this wait, the node may be doing useful work on other projects. The target itself does very little work.

### Step 3: Redirect to task self-time

Guide the user to use the **Task** Performance Summary instead:

```bash
dotnet msbuild build.binlog -noconlog -fl "-flp:v=diag;logfile=full.log;performancesummary"
grep "Task Performance Summary" -A 50 full.log
```

Focus on self-time of actual tasks:

- **Csc**: see `build-perf-diagnostics` skill (Section 2: Roslyn Analyzers)
- **ResolveAssemblyReference**: see `build-perf-diagnostics` skill (Section 1: RAR)
- **Copy**: see `build-perf-diagnostics` skill (Section 4: File I/O)
- **Serialization bottlenecks**: see `build-parallelism` skill

## Validation

- [ ] Task Performance Summary was used instead of Target Performance Summary
- [ ] `ResolveProjectReferences` was not set as the optimization target
- [ ] A concrete task (e.g., `Csc`, `Copy`, `ResolveAssemblyReference`) was identified as the true bottleneck

## When Not to Use

data.description: Guide for interpreting ResolveProjectReferences time in MSBuild performance summaries. Only activate in MSBuild/.NET build context. Activate when ResolveProjectReferences appears as the most expensive target and developers are trying to optimize it directly. Explains that the reported time includes wait time for dependent project builds and is misleading. Guides users to focus on task self-time instead. data.content:
**ResolveProjectReferences is NOT your real bottleneck.** This is a very common misdiagnosis.

### Why the 70% number is misleading

The Target Performance Summary reports **wall-clock time**, which for `ResolveProjectReferences` includes **waiting for dependent projects (LibA, LibB) to finish building**. During that wait, the MSBuild node is yielded and may be doing useful work on other projects. The target itself does almost no CPU work.

Your dependency chain is: `App → LibB → LibA`, so App must wait for both libraries. That wait time is reported *as if* it's the target's own cost.

### What you should do instead

Look at the **Task Performance Summary** (not Target) to find the actual expensive work. Generate a binlog and extract it:

```bash
dotnet build App.csproj /bl:build.binlog
dotnet msbuild build.binlog -noconlog -fl \
  "-flp:v=diag;logfile=full.log;performancesummary"
grep "Task Performance Summary" -A 50 full.log
```

The real bottlenecks will be tasks like **Csc** (compilation), **ResolveAssemblyReference**, or **Copy** — those have meaningful self-time.

### Don't do any of the three ideas in your report

| Idea | Why it's wrong |
|---|---|
| Replace ProjectReferences with NuGet packages | Adds packaging complexity for zero gain; the *compilation* time doesn't change |
| Skip ResolveProjectReferences via override | Will break the build — downstream projects won't get their dependency outputs |
| Consolidate into one project | Eliminates parallelism and makes incremental builds worse |

### Summary

Treat `ResolveProjectReferences` as a scheduling artifact, not an optimization target. Run the binlog analysis above to find which **tasks** actually consume CPU, then optimize those.
